content.assessment: Through the Polish reform lens, status quo maintenance represents an absence of structural mechanism entirely — no change to sorting points, no distributional intervention, and thus no reversibility risk because nothing is being reversed. But this is precisely the lens's diagnostic weakness: Poland's own history shows that structural inertia is not neutral. The pre-1999 Polish system's early tracking was itself the problem that reform addressed; standing still preserves the structural mechanism that produces the distributional effects documented in Hungary. The lens's implementation-sequence question is moot here since no implementation occurs, but this also means no implementation-cost lessons from Poland's reversal apply — there is no disruption to sequence.
opportunities: Monitoring infrastructure could in principle detect deterioration early enough to inform a future, better-sequenced reform than Poland's abrupt 2017 reversal
strengths: Avoids the severe implementation costs (school closures, job losses, double-cohort overcrowding) that Poland's 2017 reversal demonstrates can accompany structural change
No reversibility risk since no structural mechanism is altered
threats: Polish precedent shows that delaying structural reform does not make future reform easier or less disruptive when it eventually occurs — deferred reorganization does not avoid implementation costs, only postpones them
weaknesses: Preserves the exact structural mechanism (early selection age) that Poland's 1999 reform targeted and that produced measured PISA and labour-market gains when removed
Distributional effects (segregation, selection intensity) are left fully in place with no corrective mechanism, unlike any of the alternative proposals
